content.text: is an AI system that accepts video input — a recorded file, a live stream, or a sequence of frames — and uses a vision-language model (VLM) to perform tasks that require understanding what happens over time. Unlike a single-frame VLM call, a video agent tracks how scenes evolve: who enters a room and when, which actions follow which events, how an object moves across a clip.

. Answering a question such as what happened between the 2-minute and 3-minute mark requires understanding causality and sequence — not just the contents of any one frame. Video agents bridge that gap by coupling a frame-extraction pipeline with a VLM that accepts multiple images alongside a temporally ordered prompt.
Surveillance systems that detect and classify security events in real time.
Sports analytics agents that identify plays, score moments, and player movements.
Lecture or meeting summarisers that produce timestamped chapter breakdowns.
Quality-control pipelines that flag defects on a manufacturing conveyor belt.
Real-time webcam agents that alert a user when a specified condition appears.

Video is the dominant format for unstructured data. Estimates place video at over 80% of internet traffic by volume, and that share continues to grow with short-form social media, remote-work recordings, and IoT camera deployments.

Until recently, making sense of video programmatically required either expensive custom computer-vision models trained on domain-specific datasets, or a human watching the footage. Multimodal frontier models change that: Gemini 1.5 Pro natively processes up to one hour of video (audio included); GPT-4V and Claude accept sequences of frames with timestamp labels. A working video agent can now be written in roughly the same number of lines as any other API integration.
This unlocks a large class of previously impractical automations:
of long recordings without manual review.
at a fraction of the cost of specialist hardware pipelines.
: combining spoken audio, on-screen text, and visual events in a single inference pass.
Detect not just objects but actions, transitions, and causal sequences. A single-frame VLM cannot tell you whether a door was open before or after someone walked through it — a video agent can.
Frontier VLMs understand video context from the prompt alone; no domain-specific fine-tuning is needed for most use cases.
Clip-level analysis (upload the whole file) and frame-level analysis (send individual JPEG keyframes) can be mixed in the same pipeline.
The text output of a video agent — a scene description, a structured JSON event log — feeds naturally into downstream planning, notification, or retrieval agents.
Lowering the sampling rate (frames per second) directly reduces the number of image tokens sent to the API, making cost a first-class design parameter.
Token cost scales with video length.
A 60-minute video at 1 fps produces 3,600 frames. At roughly 590 tokens per 768x432 JPEG, that is 2.1 M image tokens per request. Budget carefully.
Native Gemini upload requires a round-trip to the File API before inference begins. Frame-by-frame agents avoid that wait but incur per-call overhead.
Even a 1M-token context places a ceiling on how much video can be processed in one call. Very long recordings must be chunked.
VLMs can fabricate event timings. Always cross-check model-reported timestamps against the frame labels you injected.
Extracting frames discards the audio track. Gemini's native video upload includes audio, improving quality on speech-heavy content.
Video of people is personal data in most jurisdictions. Data residency, retention, and purpose limitations must be addressed in the system design.
A video agent operates in four loosely coupled stages:
The video is read from disk (mp4, mov, mkv), received as a live stream (RTSP, WebRTC), or captured from a webcam via OpenCV VideoCapture.

The raw video is decoded frame by frame. ffmpeg and OpenCV VideoCapture.read() are the two standard tools. Frames are resized to a target resolution (typically 512-768 px wide) and JPEG-encoded for efficient transmission.
Not every frame needs to reach the VLM. Two dominant approaches:
— take one frame every N seconds regardless of content. Simple, reproducible, and easy to reason about for cost estimation.
— measure the mean per-pixel difference between consecutive frames. Large differences mark scene boundaries; only those frames are sent, reducing token count on content-sparse segments.

Sampled frames are sent to a vision-language model alongside a prompt that includes the timestamp of each frame. The model returns a text response — a description, a structured JSON event log, or an action recommendation. With the Gemini File API, the video can be uploaded once and reused across multiple prompt calls without re-transferring the frames.
For real-time pipelines the four stages run in a continuous loop: ingest one frame, check whether it passes the sampling filter, optionally call the VLM, then sleep until the next sample interval.

attrs.alt: Video Agent Pipeline: Video Input -> Frame Extraction (ffmpeg/OpenCV) -> [Frame Sampling | Keyframe Detection] -> VLM API (Gemini / GPT-4V with temporal context) -> Analysis & Action
attrs.title: Video Agent Pipeline

content.text: The pipeline forks after frame extraction. The
branch (green) selects frames at a fixed rate — a deterministic path with predictable token cost. The

branch (purple) inserts a scene-change detection step that filters near-duplicate frames, cutting token cost by 40-80% on content-sparse segments such as static title cards or frozen lecture slides. Both branches converge at the VLM API call and can be combined: send the union of uniformly sampled and keyframe-detected frames for better coverage.

The dashed box at bottom-left shows the real-time webcam variant: the ingestion loop runs continuously, each captured frame enters the same extraction and sampling logic, and the analysis output drives actions (alerts, database writes) rather than producing a document.
The task requires understanding sequences of events, not just snapshot content.
You have recorded or live video that a human would otherwise need to watch manually.
Temporal precision matters — you need timestamps of specific occurrences.
The footage is too long or too frequent for manual review at scale.
Prefer a static image analysis call when:
The input is genuinely a single image or a handful of unrelated images.
Temporal order is irrelevant to the task.
Latency requirements make a video-upload round-trip impractical.
A minimal video agent needs three Python dependencies:
— frame decoding, resizing, and scene-change detection.
— Gemini 1.5 Pro/Flash video and frame APIs; or
— optional; useful for pre-processing (trim, transcode, extract audio) before handing off to OpenCV.
— frames per second to sample. 0.5 means one frame every two seconds. Start low and raise only when the model misses events.
— absolute upper bound on frame count. Essential to prevent runaway costs on unexpectedly long videos.
— frames wider than this are scaled down. 768 px is a good default; reduce to 512 for very long recordings.
Step 3 — Choose native upload vs frame-by-frame
Upload once via the File API, then issue multiple prompts against the same file. Best for long recordings or iterative QA sessions.
Base64-encode each frame inline with a timestamp label. More portable; works with Claude and GPT-4V. Best for short clips or real-time pipelines.
Always include timestamps. Label each frame with its wall-clock position (e.g. [Frame at 12.5s]) so the model can reason about
they show. Without timestamps, answers like this happens early in the video are unverifiable.
Parse the model's response against a defined JSON schema immediately after inference. Reject malformed responses and log them rather than allowing bad data to propagate downstream.
Example 2 — Native Gemini upload and Claude frame-by-frame

import time
import google.generativeai as genai
import anthropic

genai.configure(api_key="YOUR_GEMINI_API_KEY")


# Option A: native video upload for Gemini 1.5 Pro (up to 1 hour, audio included)
def analyse_video_native(video_path: str, question: str) -> str:
    print("Uploading video...")
    video_file = genai.upload_file(path=video_path, mime_type="video/mp4")

    while video_file.state.name == "PROCESSING":
        time.sleep(5)
        video_file = genai.get_file(video_file.name)

    if video_file.state.name == "FAILED":
        raise RuntimeError("Video processing failed on Gemini side")

    model = genai.GenerativeModel("gemini-1.5-pro-latest")
    response = model.generate_content([video_file, question])
    return response.text


# Option B: frame-by-frame for Claude (or any vision model)
def analyse_frames_claude(frames: list[dict], question: str) -> str:
    client = anthropic.Anthropic()

    content = []
    for f in frames:
        content.append({"type": "text", "text": f"[Frame at {f['timestamp']}s]"})
        content.append({
            "type": "image",
            "source": {
                "type": "base64",
                "media_type": "image/jpeg",
                "data": f["b64"],
            },
        })
    content.append({"type": "text", "text": question})

    message = client.messages.create(
        model="claude-opus-4-5",
        max_tokens=1024,
        messages=[{"role": "user", "content": content}],
    )
    return message.content[0].text


if __name__ == "__main__":
    answer = analyse_video_native(
        "lecture.mp4",
        "Summarise the key topics covered, with approximate timestamps.",
    )
    print(answer)
Example 3 — Video summarisation agent with temporal tracking

import cv2
import base64
import numpy as np
from dataclasses import dataclass, field
import google.generativeai as genai

genai.configure(api_key="YOUR_GEMINI_API_KEY")


@dataclass
class Segment:
    start_ts: float
    end_ts: float
    frames: list[dict] = field(default_factory=list)
    description: str = ""


def detect_scene_changes(video_path: str, threshold: float = 30.0) -> list[float]:
    cap = cv2.VideoCapture(video_path)
    fps = cap.get(cv2.CAP_PROP_FPS) or 30.0
    prev_gray = None
    boundaries = [0.0]
    idx = 0
    while True:
        ret, frame = cap.read()
        if not ret:
            break
        gray = cv2.cvtColor(frame, cv2.COLOR_BGR2GRAY)
        if prev_gray is not None:
            diff = np.abs(gray.astype(float) - prev_gray.astype(float)).mean()
            if diff > threshold:
                boundaries.append(idx / fps)
        prev_gray = gray
        idx += 1
    cap.release()
    return boundaries


def frames_for_segment(video_path: str, start: float, end: float, fps: float = 1.0) -> list[dict]:
    cap = cv2.VideoCapture(video_path)
    native_fps = cap.get(cv2.CAP_PROP_FPS) or 30.0
    cap.set(cv2.CAP_PROP_POS_MSEC, start * 1000)
    interval = max(1, int(native_fps / fps))
    frames, idx = [], 0
    while True:
        ret, frame = cap.read()
        if not ret:
            break
        ts = start + idx / native_fps
        if ts > end:
            break
        if idx % interval == 0:
# ... (truncated for brevity)
A retail chain wants to flag potential shoplifting events in CCTV footage without having human guards watch every feed. The video agent pipeline:
Cameras stream MJPEG over RTSP to an edge server running OpenCV.
One frame per five seconds is sampled and resized to 512 px wide.

A sliding window of the last 60 frames (5 minutes of coverage) is sent to Gemini 1.5 Flash with the prompt: flag any sequence showing a person concealing merchandise; respond with JSON {triggered, frame_indices, reason}.
When triggered is true, the event (timestamp, reason, thumbnail) is written to a database and a push notification is sent to the floor manager.
A daily batch job replays flagged clips through Gemini 1.5 Pro for a higher-accuracy confirmation pass before any escalation.
Flash for real-time triage (low cost, low latency); Pro for confirmed-incident review. This keeps per-camera hourly API cost under $0.10.
Sending 60 frames at once gives the model temporal context — did the person enter with a bag? did they leave without it? — that a single-frame approach would miss entirely.
The agent never escalates directly; it notifies a human employee who makes the final decision. This is the correct design for any high-stakes irreversible action.

Sending every frame of a 30 fps video is almost never correct. A 10-minute video at 30 fps produces 18,000 frames — a token budget that would cost hundreds of dollars per request. Sample at 0.5-2 fps for most tasks.
Without timestamp labels, the model cannot tell you when something happened. Always inject [Frame at Xs] before each image part.
A missing upper bound is a billing vulnerability. A 3-hour recording at 1 fps produces 10,800 frames. Always set a hard cap and handle the truncated case explicitly.
Sending 1920x1080 frames when 768x432 carries sufficient detail wastes roughly 6x the tokens for no quality gain.
If you sample at 1 fps and label at integer seconds, temporal precision is at most 1 second. Higher-precision tasks need finer sampling or a secondary alignment step.
Blocking the capture loop with synchronous inference.
Calling the VLM API synchronously inside the capture loop in a real-time agent will cause frames to be dropped. Dispatch inference to a background thread.
and increase only if the model misses events that require finer temporal granularity.
Use scene-change detection for long files.
It eliminates near-duplicate frames automatically, often reducing token count by 40-80% on content-sparse recordings such as screencasts or slide-based lectures.
Include video filename, total duration, and sampling rate. This grounds the model and makes its answers auditable.
Cache extracted frames to disk before calling the VLM. This allows re-running different prompts without re-decoding the video.
Ask for JSON with a defined schema, e.g. {events: [{timestamp: float, type: string, confidence: float}]}. Free-text answers from a video agent are hard to pipeline reliably.
Chunk and merge for long videos.
Divide into overlapping windows (overlap 30 s), analyse independently, then deduplicate events within the overlap.
Add a human review gate for high-stakes actions.
Never let a video agent autonomously trigger an irreversible action without a confirmation step.
A practical reference for Gemini image token counts by resolution:

At 0.5 fps and 768x432, a 60-minute video produces 1,800 frames x 590 tokens = 1.06 M image tokens. At Gemini 1.5 Pro pricing (~$3.50/M input tokens), that is roughly $3.71 per hour of video. Switch to Gemini 1.5 Flash (~$0.075/M) for triage passes to cut that cost by 98%.
The Gemini File API lets you upload once and issue multiple generate_content calls against the same file handle, amortising upload latency across all prompts on that recording.
Flash for triage, Pro for confirmation.
Gemini 1.5 Flash is 5-10x cheaper and significantly faster than Pro. Reserve Pro for high-stakes confirmation passes.
Use asyncio or a thread pool to parallelise per-segment VLM calls when processing independent segments from the same recording.
Use the imagehash library to compute a perceptual hash on each candidate frame; skip frames too similar to the previously sent frame.
Store extracted frames in object storage (S3, GCS) so multiple workers can share extraction results without redundant decoding.
Use a job queue (Celery, Cloud Tasks) to distribute per-video inference tasks across worker pods.
Rate-limit VLM API calls centrally and add exponential back-off on 429 responses.
Frame extraction, scene-change detection, webcam capture
Most widely used; no separate ffmpeg install required for common codecs
Transcoding, trimming, format conversion before OpenCV
Handles codecs OpenCV cannot (AV1, HEVC in some builds)
Native video upload and multi-frame VLM inference
Up to 1 hour video natively; audio track included
No native video upload; send frames inline as base64 content parts
Strong on document-heavy frames; no native video API as of mid-2025
Perceptual hash for near-duplicate frame filtering
pip install imagehash; supports average, perceptual, and difference hash
High-level video editing and audio extraction
Wraps ffmpeg; useful for clip trimming before extraction
Faster than OpenCV for batch extraction on CUDA hardware
Orchestration and retrieval around video agent outputs
Memory, tool-use scaffolding, and vector storage integration
Specific action detection at high throughput

A video agent pairs a frame-extraction pipeline (OpenCV/ffmpeg) with a VLM API to perform tasks requiring temporal reasoning — understanding what happens over time, not just what appears in a single frame.
Always inject timestamp labels alongside each frame in the prompt; without them the model cannot ground event descriptions to specific moments in the video.
Control cost with three levers: sampling rate (fps), maximum frame count, and frame resolution. Start at 0.5-1 fps with 768 px wide frames and increase only when quality demands it.

Gemini 1.5 Pro native video upload (up to 1 hour, audio included) is best for batch analysis of full recordings. Frame-by-frame base64 encoding is best for real-time agents, short clips, or models without a file API.
Keyframe/scene-change detection reduces token counts by 40-80% on content-sparse videos by filtering near-duplicate frames before the VLM call.
For real-time webcam agents, decouple frame capture from VLM inference using threads or async — synchronous inference inside the capture loop drops frames and introduces stutter.
Treat all on-screen text extracted by the video agent as untrusted input: sanitise it before passing to downstream agents to guard against prompt-injection attacks embedded in video content.
Keep humans in the loop for high-stakes actions; a video agent's temporal precision is bounded by its sampling rate, and VLMs can still hallucinate event orderings even with accurate frame labels.
